perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein. However, neither party shall be liable for consequential, incidental, or punitive damages, regardless of foreseeability.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding liability for consequential damages is often unenforceable, especially regarding breaches of confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements in Virginia, as it could contravene statutes or established case law regarding remedies for breach of contract or tortious interference.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia law allows for recovery of damages for misappropriation of trade secrets, including consequential damages.  A blanket waiver of consequential damages may be unenforceable if it effectively eliminates remedies for a breach of the confidentiality agreement, especially if the misappropriation was willful or malicious. Adding the waiver clause contradicts the potential remedies afforded under Virginia law for violations of trade secret protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-338. Damages LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-338. Damages Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-338 . Damages. A. Except where the user of a misappropriated trade secret has made a material and prejudicial change in his position prior to having either knowledge or reason to know of the misappropriation and the court determines that a monetary recovery would be inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. If a complainant is unable to prove a greater amount of damages by other methods of measurement, the damages caused by misappropriation can be measured exclusively by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. B. If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award punitive damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection A of this section, or $350,000 whichever amount is less. 1986, c. 210; 1990, c. 344. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provis
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a waiver of liability for consequential, incidental, or punitive damages, which contradicts Virginia law regarding trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically § 59.1-338, states that a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation, including actual loss and unjust enrichment. Furthermore, it allows for punitive damages in cases of willful and malicious misappropriation. The added waiver clause in the changed text directly undermines the remedies available under Virginia law for trade secret violations, potentially eliminating the ability to recover consequential or punitive damages, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the disclosure by the parties of certain proprietary and trade secret information is necessary for the purchase of assets by INFE from IDO; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect and maintain the confidentiality of their respective trade secrets and/or other proprietary information (hereafter collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") and to control and restrict its dissemination to prevent disclosure to unauthorized parties; and
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the disclosure by the parties of certain proprietary and trade secret information is necessary for the purchase of assets by INFE from IDO; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect and maintain the confidentiality of their respective trade secrets and/or other proprietary information (hereafter collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") and to control and restrict its dissemination to prevent disclosure to unauthorized parties; and HOWEVER, IDO makes no warranty, express or implied, regarding the accuracy or completeness of any information disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a disclaimer of warranty to the information disclosed, especially regarding accuracy, weakens the protections afforded to INFE. This could limit INFE's ability to pursue legal action if the information turns out to be inaccurate or misleading, despite its proprietary nature.  This introduces legal risk in Virginia, as it might conflict with the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Virginia's statute of frauds primarily deals with contract enforceability, the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing is a principle that applies to all contracts under Virginia law.  By disclaiming any warranty regarding the accuracy of the 'Proprietary Information,' IDO could potentially violate its duty to act in good faith, especially if INFE relies on this information to its detriment.  The added disclaimer attempts to circumvent the spirit of the agreement's promise of confidentiality and protection.

Certain contracts void as to creditors and purchasers unless in writing; law governing validity of contracts creating security interests. Every contract, not in writing, made in respect to real estate or goods and chattels in consideration of marriage, or made for the conveyance or sale of real estate, or a term therein of more than five years, and, except as otherwise provided in § 8.2-402 of the Uniform Commercial Code, every bill of sale or contract for the sale of goods and chattels when the possession is allowed to remain with the seller, shall be void, both at law and in equity, as to purchasers for value and without notice and creditors; provided, however, that if any such contract or bill of sale as is mentioned in this section creates a security interest as defined in the Uniform Commercial Code, its validity and enforceability shall be governed by the provisions of that Code. perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a disclaimer, 'IDO makes no warranty, express or implied, regarding the accuracy or completeness of any information disclosed.' This weakens the original contract's protection of 'Proprietary Information' by potentially violating the implied duty of good faith. The LLM-generated law explanation correctly identifies that under Virginia law, all contracts are subject to an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses contracts in writing, the core of the contradiction lies in undermining the spirit of the agreement's promise of confidentiality and protection through the disclaimer, which creates a situation where IDO could act in bad faith by providing inaccurate information, thus contradicting the original intent of maintaining confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever by without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever by without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with reasonable diligence.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information' to 'reasonable diligence' lowers the standard of care required to protect the confidential information. This creates a legal risk because 'reasonable diligence' is a vague standard, and could be interpreted as a lower level of protection than what is required to adequately safeguard trade secrets under Virginia law. Removing the language ties it to current practice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Definition of Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Virginia law, the failure to take reasonable steps to protect a trade secret can result in its loss of protection.  By lowering the standard of care to 'reasonable diligence,' the modified contract creates uncertainty as to whether the parties are truly taking sufficient steps to maintain the secrecy of the information, potentially jeopardizing its status as a trade secret and diminishing the protections afforded by the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The change could fail the element of reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.

Short title and definitions Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information' to 'reasonable diligence' lowers the standard of care. The Law Explanation indicates that failing to take reasonable steps to protect a trade secret can result in its loss of protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and implies a duty to maintain secrecy. By changing the standard to 'reasonable diligence,' the altered text introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with the requirement to protect trade secrets, because 'reasonable diligence' might be interpreted as a lower standard than what is currently practiced, creating a risk that the parties are not taking sufficient steps to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire one (1) year from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year from three years weakens the protection afforded to the proprietary information. Depending on the nature of the trade secrets and the industry, a one-year period may be insufficient to protect the information from being reverse-engineered or otherwise misappropriated.  This could create legal risk under Virginia trade secret law, as the courts might find the shortened period unreasonable, especially if the information has a longer lifespan.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Injunctive Relief
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia law allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets.  A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to provide adequate protection, particularly if the trade secret has a longer commercial life. By significantly shortening the term, the contract could undermine the availability of injunctive relief, as a court might be less likely to issue an injunction for misappropriation occurring after the one-year period has expired. The changed term may be insufficient to protect a trade secret.

Injunctive relief LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-337. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-337 . Injunctive relief. A. Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. B. In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. C. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 1986, c. 210. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Unc
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period for proprietary information from three years to one year. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both argue that a one-year period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Virginia law, particularly considering the potential for injunctive relief as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which says, "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined...the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation." The shortened period weakens the protection, potentially undermining the ability to obtain injunctive relief for misappropriation beyond the one-year term, therefore creating a legal contradiction because a one-year term may be deemed insufficient to protect a trade secret.
